topic: Someone discovers their mother kept every school assignment
raw_output:
[story 1]

The attic air hung thick and dusty, smelling of aged paper and forgotten lavender. I was supposed to be looking for old photo albums, but my fingers kept brushing against something unusual tucked inside a cardboard box labeled ‘Miscellaneous 1998.’ It was a neat stack of meticulously organized schoolwork. Every single assignment, from the messy algebra worksheets to the beautifully cursive history essays, was there. My mother, who always seemed so scatterbrained about anything mundane, had compiled a complete archive of everything I’d ever turned in. A strange tightness coiled in my chest. I pulled out a science project from middle school, the glue still slightly tacky. Why? I walked back downstairs, my movements stiff, and found her in the kitchen, humming softly while stirring tea. When I brought up the box, her smile faltered, a quick, almost imperceptible flicker in her eyes that made my stomach clench.

[story 2]

It started with a misplaced textbook. I was looking for my calculus notes, and instead, I found a binder overflowing with completed homework from the last three years. The sheer volume of organized effort was jarring. I ran my hand over the crisp edges of a geometry proof, feeling a sudden, cold prickle on my skin. I stared at the neatly handwritten answers, wondering what hidden agenda lay beneath this meticulous collection. I retreated to my room, pacing the worn rug. The silence amplified the drumming in my ears. When I finally confronted her, the air in the living room grew heavy. Her composure, usually so effortless, fractured for a second—a brief widening of the eyes, a quick glance towards the hallway—before she smoothly redirected the conversation, her tone overly bright, like a well-rehearsed performance.

[story 3]

I found the hidden drawer tucked behind the loose wallpaper in the study. Inside, nestled between old tax documents, was a trove of my academic history. Every test, every essay, every completed project, cataloged and filed with unnerving precision. A wave of profound unease washed over me, making my palms sweat. It wasn't just that she kept them; it was the level of dedication, the almost obsessive cataloging. I picked up a poem from high school, the ink perfectly preserved. My breath hitched. I looked towards the door, waiting for some explanation, some context for this unexpected, overwhelming display of control. When she appeared, her expression was carefully neutral, a mask firmly in place, and the silence stretched between us, thick and uncomfortable.
